trajectory_summary: The call opens on a blank slate, builds to genuine peak engagement when Guadalupe volunteers four months of escalating bill data and a utility dismissal — then collapses when the rep applies a policy exit before running the diagnostic sequence that determines whether the policy applies. The call ends with no appointment, no qualification data captured, and the customer self-directing to call back 'when I have my husband answer' — a soft callback with no locked time and no structure.
outcome_dispute: v1 tag is 'unknown' — the actual outcome is a soft unstructured callback with no appointment booked and no qualification completed. The customer's parting line ('I'll call you back when I have my husband answer') is not a booked callback; it is a polite exit. No-show probability is high. The call should be tagged as 'disengaged — no appointment, soft callback only.'

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Orphaned-flow sequence discipline — run the diagnostic before applying the policy
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Gotcha. Okay. Yeah. I would definitely just check with him. The issue is is we won't be able to do anything on equipment that might not be our own.
primary_coaching_focus.why: The rep applied a real Ion policy at the wrong step, twice, to a customer who was actively asking for help. The orphaned_flow sequence (own vs. lease, company name, system size, working status) takes under two minutes and determines whether the policy applies. Skipping it means the rep may be DQ'ing Ion's own customers. This is the single highest-leverage coaching gap in the call — it explains both the lost appointment and the unstructured exit.

key_moments.quote: I got the bill the electricity bill of men for $700.
key_moments.stewart_read: Guadalupe volunteers her $700 bill unprompted. This is the bill anchor arriving as a gift — the rep did not have to extract it. The schema requires capture AND emotional flip (alignment-against-utility). The rep acknowledged with 'I don't blame you' but did not flip the figure into a value bridge. Capture happened; the flip did not.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. And another another month, it was $580. Another month was $5.20. Another 5 was $4.80.
key_moments.stewart_read: Guadalupe delivers three additional months of bill data in rapid succession — $580, $520, $480 — building a multi-month pattern of high bills despite having solar. This is the flip opportunity: four months of data, all elevated, all with solar supposedly running. The rep's response ('I don't blame you') is empathetic but drops the data entirely. The figures are never named back, never totaled, never used as leverage.

key_moments.quote: So it's ridiculous. That's too much money for electricity. And I called medical. They said, you know, that's what it is.
key_moments.stewart_read: Guadalupe has already tried to get help from her utility and was dismissed. This is a direct alignment-against-utility bridge point — the utility told her to accept the bill. The rep's response ('Yeah.') acknowledges but does not use this moment. The utility dismissal is the emotional proof that the existing system isn't working and that the utility won't fix it. It is the strongest setup for a value bridge in the call.

key_moments.quote: Gotcha. Okay. Yeah. I would definitely just check with him. The issue is is we won't be able to do anything on equipment that might not be our own.
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep applies Ion's third-party-equipment policy before running the orphaned_flow diagnostic sequence. The policy is real — Ion cannot service equipment it did not install. But the sequence exists to determine whether the policy applies: own vs. lease, company name, system size, working status. None of these questions were asked. The rep is not wrong about the policy; the rep is wrong about the sequence. This is premature policy application, not policy ignorance.
